name: Embalse de la Concepción from Marbella – circular via San Pedro de Alcántara
name_original: Embalse de la Concepción desde Marbella – circular por San Pedro de Alcántara
region_intro: Touring cycling around Marbella offers a diverse landscape, from the Mediterranean coastline to the foothills of the Sierra Blanca mountains. The region features well-maintained coastal promenades, challenging mountain ascents, and routes through picturesque inland villages. This geographical variety provides options for different cycling preferences and ability levels. Best touring cycling routes around Marbella The most popular touring cycling route is Embalse de la Concepción from Marbella – circular via San Pedro de Alcántara, a 22.6 miles (36.4 km) trail that takes 2 hours 32 minutes to complete. This moderate route offers views of the reservoir and passes through coastal areas. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Puente Romano Beach Bar – Source of the Molinos River loop from Barrio Nuevo, a difficult 29.0 miles (46.6 km) path. This route ascends towards the Molinos River, providing significant elevation gain and varied scenery. Local touring cyclists also love the Marbella Marina – Marbella Pier loop from Nueva Andalucía, a 10.9 miles (17.5 km) trail leading through coastal areas and urban promenades, often completed in about 1 hour 11 minutes. Touring cycling around Marbella is defined by coastal paths, mountain climbs into the Sierra Blanca, and routes through white villages. The network offers options for easy coastal rides as well as more challenging mountain ascents. The routes in Marbella are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.3 stars from more than 170 reviews. More than 3,800 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Marbella's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available in Marbella?
A: Marbella offers a wide variety of touring cycling routes, with over 110 options available on komoot. These routes cater to different skill levels, including easy coastal rides, moderate paths, and challenging mountain ascents.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes in Marbella?
A: Touring cycling in Marbella offers incredibly diverse terrain. You can enjoy flat, well-maintained paths along the Mediterranean Sea, such as the &lt;strong&gt;Paseo Marítimo&lt;/strong&gt;, or venture into the mountains for demanding climbs in the &lt;strong&gt;Sierra Blanca&lt;/strong&gt; and &lt;strong&gt;Sierra de las Nieves Natural Park&lt;/strong&gt;. Routes also pass through picturesque white villages and serene countryside with olive groves.

Q: Are there touring cycling routes suitable for beginners or those looking for an easy ride?
A: Yes, Marbella has several easy touring cycling routes. For a relaxed experience, the coastal promenades are ideal. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31534277" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Marbella Marina – Marbella Pier loop from Nueva Andalucía&lt;/a&gt;, which is 10.9 miles (17.5 km) long and leads through coastal areas and urban promenades.

Q: Are there challenging touring cycling routes for experienced cyclists in Marbella?
A: Absolutely. For experienced cyclists seeking a challenge, the mountains surrounding Marbella offer significant elevation gains and demanding climbs. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38205339" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puente Romano Beach Bar – Source of the Molinos River loop from Barrio Nuevo&lt;/a&gt;, which is 29.0 miles (46.6 km) with over 750 meters of ascent, provide a difficult and rewarding experience towards the Molinos River.

Q: Are there circular touring cycling routes in Marbella?
A: Yes, many touring cycling routes in Marbella are circular, allowing you to start and end at the same point. A popular circular route is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e974375070/embalse-de-la-concepcion-from-marbella-circular-via-san-pedro-de-alcantara" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Embalse de la Concepción from Marbella – circular via San Pedro de Alcántara&lt;/a&gt;, offering views of the reservoir and coastal areas over 22.6 miles (36.4 km).

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see along touring cycling routes in Marbella?
A: Marbella's routes offer a blend of natural beauty and historical sites. You can cycle along the stunning Mediterranean coastline, through the unique &lt;strong&gt;Artola Dunes&lt;/strong&gt; near Cabopino Beach, or ascend into the &lt;strong&gt;Sierra de las Nieves Natural Park&lt;/strong&gt; for sweeping mountain views. Historic sites like Marbella's &lt;strong&gt;Historic Center&lt;/strong&gt; and various castles, such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5820655" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Marbella Castle&lt;/a&gt;, can also be explored.

Q: Are there touring cycling routes that pass by lakes or reservoirs in Marbella?
A: Yes, some routes will take you past beautiful water bodies. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e974375070/embalse-de-la-concepcion-from-marbella-circular-via-san-pedro-de-alcantara" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Embalse de la Concepción from Marbella – circular via San Pedro de Alcántara&lt;/a&gt; route offers views of the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2410074" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Concepción Reservoir&lt;/a&gt;. The road towards Istán also provides stunning reservoir views amidst the mountains.

Q: What do other touring cyclists say about the routes in Marbella?
A: The touring cycling routes in Marbella are highly regarded by the komoot community, with an average rating of 4.3 stars from over 170 reviews. More than 3,800 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore the region, often praising the diverse landscapes, from tranquil coastlines to rugged mountains, and the well-developed cycling infrastructure.

Q: Are there touring cycling routes that lead to charming villages or offer cultural experiences?
A: Yes, cycling inland from Marbella allows you to explore charming white villages. Routes to places like &lt;strong&gt;Ojén&lt;/strong&gt; and &lt;strong&gt;Istán&lt;/strong&gt; offer not only challenging climbs and fantastic views but also a glimpse into traditional Andalusian life. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40356685" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Los Caracolillos – Ojén Caves loop from Playa de Levante&lt;/a&gt; is an example of a route that takes you towards Ojén.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in Marbella?
A: Marbella generally offers excellent cycling conditions for much of the year. The mild Mediterranean climate means that spring and autumn are particularly pleasant, with comfortable temperatures. Even winter can be suitable for cycling, offering a great escape from colder climates, while summer might be best for early morning or late afternoon rides to avoid the peak heat.

Q: Is it possible to access touring cycling routes using public transport in Marbella?
A: Marbella has been developing its cycling infrastructure, and while direct public transport access to all trailheads might vary, the city center and coastal areas are well-connected. Many routes start directly from urban areas, making them accessible. For specific routes, checking local bus services for proximity to starting points is advisable.

Q: Where can I find parking for touring cycling routes in Marbella?
A: Parking availability for touring cycling routes in Marbella depends on the starting point. For coastal routes, parking can often be found near beaches or promenades. For routes heading into the mountains or villages, designated parking areas are usually available in or near the starting towns. It's recommended to check specific route details for parking suggestions.
tips: Overall, the coastal path between Marbella and San Pedro de Alcántara offers a scenic and relaxing bike ride along the Costa del Sol that is a special experience. The route consists of various sections with different surfaces, including cobblestones, wooden walkways and sandy paths, all of which are easy to ride on. There are numerous opportunities for breaks along the way, whether to eat, drink or simply enjoy the view. The route is flat, passes beaches, restaurants and sights and is ideal for cycling.

San Pedro de Alcántara is a picturesque district of Marbella, located about ten kilometers west of the city center. It is a charming coastal town that has retained its Andalusian character while offering modern facilities. San Pedro is a popular destination for visitors looking for a quieter and more authentic atmosphere compared to the more glamorous parts of Marbella. San Pedro de Alcántara was founded in 1860 by the Marquis del Duero, Manuel Gutiérrez de la Concha. Originally the town was an agricultural center with sugar cane plantations and the Marquis planned it as a modern workers' colony. Today San Pedro is a thriving urban area that combines tradition and modernity.

The town offers several attractions, such as the church of San Pedro de Alcántara. This church, built in the 19th century, is located in the heart of the town and is a fine example of traditional Andalusian architecture. It is located in a charming square surrounded by small cafes. San Pedro Boulevard is a modern boulevard with impressive pedestrian bridges, green spaces, playgrounds and restaurants. An archaeological site is the Vega del Mar Basilica, which dates back to the late Roman period (4th to 6th centuries AD). It is one of the oldest early Christian churches in Spain, discovered in the middle of an ancient cemetery. San Pedro has a beautiful, quiet seafront promenade that leads all the way to Marbella. The beaches are less crowded than in other parts of Marbella and offer a relaxed ambience.

San Pedro de Alcántara stands out for its relaxed atmosphere, historical roots and less touristy appeal. It is ideal for cyclists who want to experience an authentic part of the Costa del Sol.

Puerto Banús is the luxury marina, one of the most important in the world. Opened in 1970.

One of Puerto Banus' landmarks is the statue that stands in the middle of the roundabout at the coastal end of Avenida de Nacionales Unidas. The statue, called 'La Victoria' is built of bronze and copper and stands on top of a 26m (85ft) tall column of granite. It was designed by the famous Georgian sculptor, Zurab Tsereteli, and gifted to Puerto Banus in 1994 by the Mayor of Moscow.
